title: MMAI Gym for Science: Training Liquid Foundation Models for Drug Discovery
tldr:
### Problem
Drug discovery tasks, such as molecular optimization, ADMET property prediction, retrosynthesis, drug-target activity prediction, and functional group reasoning, require specialized models that perform near expert-level and outperform larger models while maintaining efficiency and broad applicability.

### Method
A training recipe is proposed that uses supervised (SFT) and reinforcement learning (RFT) finetuning on domain-specific data to transform a general-purpose causal Language Model into a drug discovery generalist. This approach is instantiated in MMAI Gym, a structured training and evaluation environment that provides curated scientific reasoning traces across key modalities and tasks in drug discovery.

### Dataset / Benchmark
The training and evaluation are performed using a diverse set of molecular prediction tasks relevant to drug discovery and development.

### Key Findings
- The proposed method achieves competitive or state-of-the-art results across molecular prediction tasks.
- In some cases, the performance improves by up to an order of magnitude on task metrics.
- Smaller, purpose-trained foundation models outperform substantially larger general-purpose or specialist models on molecular benchmarks.

### Why It Matters
These findings suggest that domain-specific reasoning supervision can be an effective path toward robust multi-task Language Models for scientific R&D in drug discovery, offering a promising solution to the challenge of obtaining a single "generalist" model that performs competitively in diverse molecular reasoning workloads encountered in medicinal chemistry, biology, and early clinical development.

title: Designing the Haystack: Programmable Chemical Space for Generative Molecular Discovery
tldr:
### Problem
The current generative models in drug discovery treat chemical space as a fixed, implicitly learned distribution, focusing on sampling individual molecules rather than deliberately designing the space from which they are drawn.

### Method
In this study, the Discovery mode of SpaceGFN was employed to explicitly construct a Pseudo-NP chemical space within the DIY chemical space framework. Users can flexibly select building block libraries and reaction libraries according to their needs, constructing novel chemical spaces and using SpaceGFN to efficiently and purposefully explore them in search of molecules with desired properties.

### Dataset / Benchmark
The study was validated across 96 diverse drug targets.

### Key Findings
- The study demonstrated robust optimization performance while substantially expanding topological diversity under explicit synthetic constraints.
- By leveraging molecular motifs historically processed by living systems, SpaceGFN induces statistically favorable shifts in predicted metabolic and toxicological distributions while preserving pharmacological diversity.
- SpaceGFN enables reaction-consistent lead optimization by applying a curated molecular editing toolkit composed of executable synthetic transformations.

### Why It Matters
By unifying programmable chemical universe construction with flow-based exploration and reaction-level editing, SpaceGFN establishes a computational framework for the deliberate design and navigation of therapeutic chemical space, bridging generative AI, synthetic methodology, and biological design principles. This approach could lead to more efficient and targeted drug discovery, increasing the likelihood of finding successful therapeutic molecules.

title: MultiPUFFIN: A Multimodal Domain-Constrained Foundation Model for Molecular Property Prediction of Small Molecules
tldr:
### Problem
Small molecule physicochemical property prediction is a fundamental challenge in chemical engineering, pharmaceutical development, and materials science. Existing molecular foundation models achieve impressive performance through large-scale pre-training but lack thermodynamic consistency in their outputs, while domain-informed approaches have been limited to single properties and small datasets.

### Method
The authors introduce MultiPUFFIN (Multimodal Path-Unifying Foundation Fusion Interfaced Network), a domain-constrained multimodal foundation model. It handles missing modalities, recovers thermodynamically meaningful equation parameters without explicit supervision, and addresses the limitations of single-property prediction by jointly learning related properties.

### Dataset / Benchmark
The model is trained on a comprehensive multi-source dataset of 37,968 unique molecules (40,904 data rows) assembled from nine public databases, representing a 59% expansion in molecular coverage over the initial curation.

### Key Findings
- MultiPUFFIN achieves competitive test performance across a challenging scaffold-split test set of 8877 molecules, with mean R2 = 0.716 across all nine properties.
- The model's domain-constrained multimodal approach reduces the data and computational requirements for molecular property prediction, offering a more efficient alternative to brute-force pre-training at scale.
- Systematic ablation studies quantify the contribution of each architectural component and domain-informed head, revealing the property-specific nature of the benefit conferred by domain-informed prediction heads.

### Why It Matters
MultiPUFFIN addresses the limitations of existing molecular foundation models by offering a domain-constrained multimodal approach that jointly learns related properties, improving the efficiency of molecular property prediction and enabling the prediction of thermodynamically meaningful properties. This work has implications for drug discovery, computational chemistry, and molecular machine learning.

title: GlassMol: Interpretable Molecular Property Prediction with Concept Bottleneck Models
tldr:
### Problem
Machine learning-enabled molecular property prediction seeks to identify promising areas of research for wet-lab chemists, increasing the efficiency of the entire research pipeline. However, state-of-the-art Large Language Models and Graph Neural Networks operate as black boxes, which poses challenges in drug discovery, especially regarding safety and regulatory approval.

### Method
We propose GlassMol, a model-agnostic adaptation of Concept-Based Machine Learning (CBM) to molecular property prediction. GlassMol bridges the Annotation Gap by computing chemical descriptors via RDKit, addresses the Relevance Gap through LLM-guided concept selection, and empirically demonstrates that interpretability need not sacrifice performance.

### Dataset / Benchmark
Experiments are conducted on thirteen diverse chemical datasets.

### Key Findings
- GlassMol generally matches or exceeds black-box baselines, suggesting that interpretability need not come at the cost of performance.
- The explicit modeling of chemical concepts helps the model focus on specific toxicophores (structural alerts) rather than overfitting to spurious correlations in the high-dimensional latent space.
- GlassMol aligns with medicinal chemistry intuition, confirming that it can learn chemically meaningful concepts.

### Why It Matters
By leveraging GlassMol, molecular property prediction becomes more transparent, allowing for a more informed and engaged collaboration between machine learning experts and chemists. This can potentially accelerate the discovery of safe and effective therapeutics, addressing the critical need for rigorous justification in drug discovery, especially regarding safety and regulatory approval.

title: Hierarchical Molecular Representation Learning via Fragment-Based Self-Supervised Embedding Prediction
tldr:
### Problem
The problem addressed in this paper is the extraction of semantically rich representations from large pre-trained molecular structure datasets for downstream tasks in drug discovery, computational chemistry, and molecular machine learning.

### Method
The proposed method is Graph Semantic Predictive Network (GraSPNet), a hierarchical pretraining approach that learns node and fragment-level representations through multilevel message passing with masked semantic prediction at both levels.

### Dataset / Benchmark
The study was conducted on multiple molecular property prediction benchmarks, including SIDER, ClinTox, BBP, BACE, and Dataset5560-85.

### Key Findings
- GraSPNet learns chemically meaningful representations and outperforms state-of-the-art GSSL methods in transfer learning settings.
- By introducing fragmentation based on both geometric and chemical structure, GraSPNet constructs higher-level graph abstractions that are expressive yet maintain a lower vocabulary size, promoting better generalization.
- GraSPNet jointly predicts node and fragment embeddings to capture semantically meaningful graph representations, which is a significant improvement over methods focusing on node-or edge-level information.

### Why It Matters
This work matters for drug discovery, computational chemistry, and molecular machine learning as it provides a novel approach for extracting semantically rich representations from large molecular structure datasets. By capturing both local and higher-level semantics, GraSPNet can potentially improve the performance of downstream tasks, such as property prediction and molecular design. The hierarchical pretraining method proposed can serve as a valuable foundation for future research in graph representation learning for molecular data.

title: Rigidity-Aware Geometric Pretraining for Protein Design and Conformational Ensembles
tldr:
### Problem
The challenge lies in the requirement for diverse, multi-scale data, including conformational ensembles derived from molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, to support generative modeling with improved conformational diversity in structure-based pretraining for drug discovery and molecular machine learning.

### Method
Proposed is RigidSSL (Rigidity-Aware SelfSupervised Learning), a two-stage geometric pretraining framework for protein structure generation. This framework models a protein chain as a sequence of rigid residues, where each residue is parameterized by a translation and a rotation.

### Dataset / Benchmark
The method utilizes 432K structures from the AlphaFold Protein Structure Database with simulated perturbations (Phase I: RigidSSL-Perturb) and 1.3K molecular dynamics trajectories (Phase II: RigidSSL-MD).

### Key Findings
- RigidSSL-Perturb improves the success rate by 5.8% in zero-shot motif scaffolding.
- RigidSSL-MD captures more biophysically realistic conformational ensembles in G protein-coupled receptor modeling.
- In ensemble generation, RigidSSL variants learn to represent a wider and more physically realistic conformational landscape of G protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs), achieving the best performance on 7 out of 9 metrics.

### Why It Matters
RigidSSL addresses the challenges in learning geometry and generation simultaneously, improving model generalization and capturing a wider and more physically realistic conformational landscape for drug discovery, computational chemistry, and molecular machine learning tasks.

title: Benchmarking GNN Models on Molecular Regression Tasks with CKA-Based Representation Analysis
tldr:
### Problem
Molecular property prediction is a critical task in drug discovery, computational and analytical chemistry, requiring efficient and accurate models to handle the complexity of extensive chemical spaces. The effectiveness of these models heavily relies on expert-driven feature engineering, which can be time-consuming and may struggle with limited experimental data.

### Method
The study introduces a method called MvMRL (Multi-view Molecular Representation Learning) for molecular property prediction. This method bypasses the need for manual hand-crafted features by using Graph Neural Networks (GNN) to learn hierarchical topological features and hidden structural patterns directly from molecular graphs.

### Dataset / Benchmark
Three datasets, ESOL, Lipophilicity, and FreeSolv, were downloaded from the MoleculeNet database for this research.

### Key Findings
- The study reveals how these models converge and diverge in their internal logic, offering novel insights into model selection for molecular machine learning.
- The findings reveal the ability of GNN to learn inherent structural relationships within a molecule, bypassing the need for fixed-size fingerprints.
- The extent to which different GNN layers learn overlapping versus complementary representations is under-investigated, particularly for the small datasets commonly encountered in biological drug discovery.

### Why It Matters
This work is significant for drug discovery, computational chemistry, and molecular machine learning as it presents a method for efficient molecular property prediction that bypasses the need for manual feature engineering and can handle small datasets common in biological drug discovery. Additionally, the findings provide insights into model selection and the learning capabilities of GNN for molecular structures.

title: MolFM-Lite: Multi-Modal Molecular Property Prediction with Conformer Ensemble Attention and Cross-Modal Fusion
tldr:
### Problem
Molecular property prediction models often rely on a single molecular representation and treat molecular geometry as static, neglecting the intrinsic multi-scale nature of molecules.

### Method
The proposed method, MolFM-Lite, is a multi-modal model that fuses SELFIES sequences (1D), molecular graphs (2D), and conformer ensembles (3D) through cross-attention, with Feature-wise Linear Modulation (FiLM) for context conditioning.

### Dataset / Benchmark
The model is evaluated on four MoleculeNet scaffold-split benchmarks using its own splits, and baselines are re-evaluated under the same protocol.

### Key Findings
- MolFM-Lite demonstrates improved performance compared to baseline models (ChemBERTa, GIN, GROVER, SchNet) on four MoleculeNet benchmark datasets.
- The model's conformer ensemble attention mechanism, which combines learnable attention with Boltzmann-weighted thermodynamic priors over multiple RDKit-generated conformers, contributes to capturing the thermodynamic distribution of molecular shapes.
- Cross-modal fusion allows for the sharing of complementary information among the three modalities (1D, 2D, 3D).

### Why It Matters
MolFM-Lite's ability to effectively handle multiple molecular representations and conformational flexibility addresses a significant limitation in existing machine learning models for molecular property prediction. This advancement could lead to more accurate predictions, improved drug discovery, and better understanding of molecular properties and behaviors.

title: MACE-POLAR-1: A Polarisable Electrostatic Foundation Model for Molecular Chemistry
tldr:
### Problem
Computational molecular chemistry often requires high levels of electronic structure theory, which is computationally expensive, particularly for large and diverse datasets. Accurate modeling of electrostatic interactions, crucial in drug discovery and molecular behavior, remains a central challenge.

### Method
The proposed method extends the MACE architecture with an explicit field-dependent induction mechanism for long-range interactions. This model learns complex relationships between local atomic environments and their contributions to total energy, providing interpretable spin-resolved charge densities and handling variable charge, spin states, and external fields.

### Dataset / Benchmark
The model's ability is evaluated on various benchmarks, including molecular crystal lattice energies, protein–ligand binding, and other challenging systems.

### Key Findings
- The inclusion of physics-based electrostatics improves the description of charged systems and non-covalent complexes, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy.
- The model demonstrates versatility across a broad chemical space, from small molecules to protein–ligand complexes.
- Long-range interactions, critical for charged molecules, ionic materials, and large biomolecular complexes, are accurately modeled.

### Why It Matters
This work offers a significant advancement in computational molecular chemistry and drug discovery by providing a versatile tool that can model electrostatic interactions with near–ab initio accuracy at a fraction of the computational cost. This democratizes the use of Machine Learning Interatomic Potentials (MLIPs) for a wide range of chemists.

title: Bayesian Optimization in Chemical Compound Sub-Spaces using Low-Dimensional Molecular Descriptors
tldr:
### Problem
ML models in drug discovery and molecular machine learning face the challenge of optimizing molecular properties in high-dimensional chemical compound spaces, especially when data are scarce. The non-smooth nature of the objective landscape due to the discrete nature of molecules further complicates the problem.

### Method
The proposed method is Bayesian optimization (BO) applied to chemical compound spaces using low-dimensional, interpretable descriptors to enable data-efficient optimization and robust inverse molecular design.

### Dataset / Benchmark
Not specified in the provided text.

### Key Findings
- The work demonstrates the feasibility of Bayesian optimization in chemical compound spaces, showing potential for efficient optimization of molecular properties with a small number of confirmations.
- Low-dimensional descriptors are crucial for data-efficient optimization and robust inverse molecular design.
- The study establishes Bayesian optimization as a practical tool for molecular discovery in small-data regimes.

### Why It Matters
This work addresses a significant challenge in drug discovery, computational chemistry, and molecular machine learning: the need for efficient, data-driven methods to optimize molecular properties in high-dimensional spaces. By demonstrating the feasibility of Bayesian optimization in chemical compound spaces, this research could lead to accelerated molecular discovery, particularly in situations where high-quality data are scarce.

title: Deep learning-guided evolutionary optimization for protein design
tldr:
### Problem
Sequential optimization of protein sequences for specific characteristics is computationally expensive due to the requirement of many evaluations, particularly when structure prediction or docking simulations are involved.

### Method
BoGA (Bayesian Optimization Genetic Algorithm) combines evolutionary search with Bayesian optimization in an online learning loop, using a surrogate model to approximate the objective function and guide the search towards promising regions of sequence space.

### Dataset / Benchmark
The performance of BoGA is demonstrated on sequence and structure-level optimization tasks, and subsequently applied to the design of peptide binders against pneumolysin.

### Key Findings
- Larger proposal pools accelerate discovery of high-scoring binders.
- BoGA's optimization trajectories show improved discovery of high-confidence binders compared to smaller proposal pools.
- The fitness distribution becomes bimodal as optimization proceeds, likely indicating selection of both high-confidence, high-scoring binders and low-confidence binders that score highly under expected improvement.

### Why It Matters
By combining Bayesian optimization and genetic algorithms, BoGA offers an efficient method for the design of peptide binders against biologically relevant targets. This accelerates the identification of high-confidence candidates, which can potentially lead to next-generation therapeutics and biotechnology advancements.

title: Conformal Graph Prediction with Z-Gromov Wasserstein Distances
tldr:
### Problem
Evaluation of a conformal prediction framework for supervised graph prediction, particularly in the context of molecule identification, where experimental validation is costly.

### Method
Adoption of the Agnostic, distribution free, and post-training framework of Conformal Prediction (CP) with Z-Gromov–Wasserstein (Z-GW) distance, a metric on metric measure spaces, for graph-valued outputs.

### Dataset / Benchmark
Evaluated on a synthetic image-to-graph task and a real molecule prediction problem.

### Key Findings
- Proposed a framework for Conformal Graph Prediction based on Z-GW non-conformity scores, with validity in quotiented graph spaces.
- Demonstrated the effectiveness and versatility of the framework on the synthetic and real tasks.
- Presented conformal prediction sets that provide user confidence, reducing the risk for discovery.

### Why It Matters
This work addresses the challenges in graph-valued prediction and uncertainty quantification, which are crucial for drug discovery, computational chemistry, and molecular machine learning, especially when experimental validation is costly. The proposed method can lead to more reliable and efficient predictions, thereby enhancing the accuracy of molecular identification.

title: Learning Complex Physical Regimes via Coverage-oriented Uncertainty Quantification: An application to the Critical Heat Flux
tldr:
### Problem
Machine learning (ML) models are used to predict complex physical systems with multi-regime behaviors. However, the reliability and interpretability of these models, especially in high-stakes scientific applications, are a concern due to their black-box nature.

### Method
This study contrasts post-hoc methods, such as conformal prediction (CP), with end-to-end coverage-oriented pipelines, including (Bayesian) heteroscedastic regression and quality-driven loss functions. The study uses Residual Networks for Physics Surrogate Models and tests these methods on the prediction of the Critical Heat Flux (CHF), a challenging benchmark for multi-regime scientific ML.

### Dataset / Benchmark
The study uses the Nuclear Regulatory Commission Dataset, which represents a rigorous test due to the highly non-linear dependence of CHF on the inputs and the existence of distinct microscopic physical regimes.

### Key Findings
- Post-hoc methods ensure rigorous statistical calibration, but coverage-oriented learning yields models that are physically consistent and robust by effectively reshaping the model’s representation to match the complex physical regimes.
- Uncertainty quantification (UQ) is critical for assessing the trustworthiness and physical consistency of the learned representation, guiding the model to internalise the underlying behavior of the data.

### Why It Matters
This work is significant for drug discovery, computational chemistry, and molecular machine learning as it demonstrates the importance of UQ in scientific ML. By improving the physical consistency and robustness of ML models, this research can lead to more reliable predictions and better understanding of complex physical phenomena.

title: An Active Learning Framework for Data-Efficient, Human-in-the-Loop Enzyme Function Prediction
tldr:
### Problem
Human-in-the-loop (HITL) active learning is not yet applied to enzyme discovery and function prediction, which require costly and time-consuming experimental characterization.

### Method
The study introduces HATTER, a first-ever framework for HITL enzyme function prediction. It integrates multiple active learning (AL) strategies to select the most informative unannotated protein sequences with human-in-the-loop input of novel experimentally labeled proteins.

### Dataset / Benchmark
The study evaluates HATTER's performance on large-scale enzyme function prediction under realistic sequence and label-stratified settings.

### Key Findings
- HATTER achieves statistically comparable predictive performance to standard supervised training while requiring substantially less training data.
- HITL workflows reduce labeling costs and computational effort through faster convergence, fewer model updates, and processing less data.
- HATTER is designed for iterative experimental deployment, enabling biologists to query, annotate, and update enzyme function prediction models across sequential experimental rounds.

### Why It Matters
By bridging the gap between static benchmark evaluation and real-world HITL enzyme discovery, HATTER has the potential to create biological AI tools that are not only predictive but adaptive and collaborative. This could accelerate functional discovery in uncharted regions of sequence space, reducing experimental and computational cost while maintaining competitive predictive performance.
